claim: YOLO demand evidence dispatch implements provider-agnostic boundary enforcement with provider-specific invocation logic, but lacks real-world stress testing against actual Claude -p agent behavior.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots implement provider-agnostic file system monitoring that detects project mutations regardless of which provider is used.
evidence.why: This is the core boundary protection that prevents provider-specific assumptions from causing project drift.

evidence.summary: Boundary snapshots taken before and after agent execution detect any project file mutations, with allowed write roots excluded from violation detection.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary enforcement is runtime-verified, not just declarative.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation has separate code paths for codex (lines 43-61), custom (lines 63-75), and claude (lines 77-108), each with provider-specific argument handling.
evidence.why: Provider-specific assumptions are explicit in the invocation logic, creating potential for drift if not tested.

evidence.summary: Claude provider supports claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools flags for tool capability control, while codex uses only model and sandbox settings.
evidence.why: Different providers have different tool capability interfaces, creating cross-provider inconsistency risk.

evidence.summary: executionConfig normalizes provider settings and clears Claude-specific tool flags when provider is claude, setting claude_no_session_persistence to true and agent_tool_profile based on request profile.
evidence.why: Provider-specific configuration normalization happens before dispatch, but depends on correct provider detection.

evidence.summary: test 'codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox' verifies codex provider isolation from claude configuration defaults.
evidence.why: Test evidence that provider cross-contamination is a recognized risk and is explicitly tested.

evidence.summary: test 'claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement' verifies claude provider clears tool flags and depends on runtime boundary checks.
evidence.why: Evidence that tool capability preservation relies on boundary enforcement rather than upfront tool filtering.

evidence.summary: test 'boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass' proves boundary enforcement is independent of agent success and provider used.
evidence.why: Critical evidence that boundary protection is provider-agnostic and enforced regardless of agent behavior.

evidence.summary: Git-backed boundary tests detect untracked and gitignored file mutations, proving boundary works across git and non-git scenarios.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary detection is robust across different project states, not dependent on git status.

evidence.summary: extractJsonObject includes Claude-specific JSON repair logic for line range formatting, key escaping, and trailing comma cleanup, acknowledging provider output drift.
evidence.why: Evidence that provider-specific output quirks are already handled, but only for Claude, not codex or custom providers.

evidence.summary: All provider execution tests use fake/mock agent implementations rather than real Claude -p subprocess spawns, meaning test coverage does not verify actual provider behavior.
evidence.why: Critical gap: test coverage is synthetic and does not stress-test against real Claude agent behavior.

evidence.summary: Agent prompt explicitly tells providers 'do not modify target project files' and defines allowed artifact roots, but relies on provider compliance rather than technical enforcement.
evidence.why: Evidence that provider compliance is assumed, not technically guaranteed, creating provider trust risk.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents will respect the do-not-modify-project-files instruction in the prompt.
Provider-specific tool flags (claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools) are sufficient to preserve full tool capability under harness boundary.
Custom provider implementations will not have different tool capability semantics than Claude or Codex.
Boundary snapshot detection will work correctly across all operating systems and file system types.
JSON repair logic handles all provider output quirks, not just Claude-specific formatting.
risks: A real Claude -p agent might ignore the boundary instruction and modify project files, but boundary snapshot would detect this.
Provider-specific tool flag differences could cause tool capability drift between Claude, Codex, and custom providers.
Custom provider implementations might not honor the same tool capability semantics as documented provider behavior.
Test coverage using mock agents might not catch real-world provider behavior differences.
Cross-provider model leakage could occur if provider detection logic fails or config.ai normalization is incorrect.
missing: Real-world stress tests using actual Claude -p agent executions against live demand scenarios.
Evidence that custom provider implementations honor the same boundary expectations as Claude/Codex.
Validation that provider detection logic correctly identifies providers in all config scenarios.
Evidence that tool capability flags are correctly passed through to all providers without semantic drift.
Testing of boundary snapshot behavior on non-POSIX file systems (Windows, network drives).

result.notes: Evidence confirms provider-agnostic boundary enforcement exists and is well-tested synthetically. Provider-specific assumptions are explicit in code but not validated against real provider behavior. Cross-check recommended to verify provider isolation assumptions and tool capability preservation claims.
